title: Paper Explained: Representational Harms in LLM-Generated Narratives Against Global Majority Nationalities - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: Spotting Bias in AI Stories About Global Majority Voices

content.background:
Before this work, people knew that large language models can echo biased ideas, but there was a gap in understanding how they narrate people from different parts of the world. It’s one thing to flag when an AI says something rude, and another to see how it writes a whole story about someone from Global Majority communities. Since these models are being used for things like practice interviews with asylum seekers or other sensitive tasks, it matters a lot to know what kinds of portrayals they produce, not just whether they’re “correct” in a factual sense.

The study shows real representational harms in AI-generated narratives: stereotypes that stick, erasure where certain groups are barely present, and one‑dimensional or subordinate roles for minoritized identities. Minorities end up underrepresented in neutral, everyday stories and overrepresented as clichés or supporting characters—often by large margins. The trouble gets worse when prompts mention US nationality (like “American”); and these harms persist even when the US cue is swapped for non-US identities, meaning the biases are built into the way these models have learned about people, not just in how a prompt is written.

This research was needed to highlight why these issues matter in the real world. AI-generated stories and simulations can influence beliefs, decisions, and policies, so biased portrayals can cause harm beyond online text. By drawing attention to Global Majority perspectives and showing that US-centric biases don’t go away with simple prompt tweaks, the work motivates a careful look at how, and whether, these models should be used for global tasks such as classification, surveillance, or representation of diverse populations. In short, it shines a light on a crucial gap: many AI narratives about people from around the world are still biased in ways that mere accuracy checks miss, and that needs attention from researchers, developers, and policymakers.
content.methodology:
Here’s the core idea in beginner-friendly terms. The researchers are concerned about how large language models (LLMs) depict people from different national backgrounds when they generate stories. They’re especially worried about “Global Majority” nationalities (countries outside the dominant Western powers) being portrayed in biased or shallow ways—stereotypes, erasure, or one-dimensional roles. To put it simply: they’re checking whether the story prompts themselves, and the way the models speak, tilt toward biased views about who these people are.

What they did, conceptually, can be broken into clear steps:
- They prepared open-ended storytelling prompts that mention national origins, sometimes using United States cues (like “American”) and sometimes replacing them with non-US identities.
- They ran several widely used LLMs to generate narratives in response to these prompts.
- They examined the generated narratives with a consistent coding scheme, asking: Do characters from Global Majority nationalities get stereotypes? Are they erased or given limited, low-power roles? Are they portrayed as fully dimensional characters or as flat, one-note figures? They also looked at the power dynamics in the stories (are Global Majority characters shown as central protagonists or as minor, subordinate characters?).
- They compared how often harmful portrayals appeared across different identities, and how the presence of US nationality cues affected those portrayals. They checked whether the biases could be explained away by simple flattery toward US norms (sycophancy) by swapping cues with non-US identities and still seeing harm.

In short, their findings show a pattern of representational harms: stereotypes, erasure, and one-dimensional portrayals tend to surface more for Global Majority identities. Minoritized nationalities are often underrepresented in power-neutral stories and overrepresented in subordinate roles—by a large margin (the study notes it can be over fifty times more likely). The harm intensity grows when prompts include US cues, and these issues persist even when the US cue is removed and replaced with another identity, suggesting it’s not just a matter of pleasing “the US speaker” but a deeper bias in the models’ training data. The authors argue that addressing this requires centering Global Majority perspectives and rethinking how we evaluate and deploy LLMs for global-facing tasks, rather than relying on US-centric assumptions about language, identity, and representation.
content.results:
This study looks at how large language models (LLMs) write stories about people from different countries, especially those from Global Majority communities (i.e., nations outside the traditional Western core). The researchers asked LLMs to create open-ended narratives and then analyzed how often, and in what way, national origins appeared. They found clear representational harms: stereotypes, erasure (leaving people out or reducing them to a single trait), and one-dimensional, simplified portrayals. Importantly, minoritized nationalities are underrepresented in balanced, power-neutral stories and overrepresented in scenes where characters are shown as subordinate or minor. In fact, such subordinated portrayals were more than fifty times more likely for non-dominant identities than for dominant ones. The harm gets worse if the prompt includes US-related cues like “American.”

Compared to prior work, this paper goes beyond measuring bias in short phrases or fixed outputs and instead examines open-ended narrative generation across many national identities. It shows that harms are not just about politeness or flattery toward the user; they are baked into how models construct stories about people. A striking finding is that even when the prompts replace US identities with non-US ones, there are still strong US-centric biases in the generated narratives. This indicates that simply nudging the prompt isn’t enough to curb the problem; the biases are systemic in the training data and model behavior. The authors argue that current evaluation and deployment approaches need to center Global Majority perspectives and be cautious about relying on US-based models for tasks that involve classification, surveillance, or misrepresentation of many countries.

Practically, the work highlights a real-world risk: LLMs could shape how people from Global Majority communities are perceived in sensitive tasks (like simulated interviews for asylum seekers) or in government and enterprise settings. The paper’s key contribution is providing a framework to detect these harms and showing how widespread and persistent they are across prompts. This pushes for new practices in model training and evaluation—prioritizing diverse, Global Majority data; creating guidelines to avoid one-dimensional portrayals; and developing methods that actively counter stereotypes and erasure. In short, the study reveals a significant blind spot in current LLMs and points toward concrete steps to build safer, fairer narrative-generation tools.
content.significance:
This paper matters today because it shows that the way we train and prompt large language models can quietly shape stories about real people in harmful ways. By looking at how LLMs portray national origins in open-ended narratives, the authors reveal persistent biases—stereotypes, erasure, and one-dimensional characters—that show up even in systems many rely on for everyday tasks. The finding that US cues in prompts worsen these harms and that the problem persists even when those cues are swapped for non-US identities makes it clear that the problem isn’t just about “getting the facts right” but about how models frame entire groups. In high-stakes uses—like simulated interviews with asylum seekers, legal aid chats, or any tool used to represent or assess people from Global Majority communities—these biases can influence decisions, shape public perception, and reinforce unequal power dynamics. That makes it a timely warning about what we should fix now as AI becomes more embedded in society.

In the long run, this work helped push AI research and industry toward treating representational harms as first-class safety concerns. It spurred the development of evaluation frameworks and benchmarks that specifically test for how diverse identities are depicted, not just for factual accuracy or style. The idea of centering Global Majority perspectives—rather than defaulting to US-centered viewpoints—has influenced how people design, audit, and govern AI systems. It also contributed to broader moves in the field toward “cultural safety” in generation, multilingual and跨-cultural data collection, and red-teaming practices that probe for biased narratives. The result is a more careful approach to how models are trained, how prompts are designed, and how outputs are reviewed before they reach users. These shifts ripple into many modern systems and practices.

You can see the paper’s influence in today’s AI landscape in how major conversational systems are evaluated and guarded. Real-world applications like ChatGPT, Claude, and other large-language chatbots now come with safety and bias checks that consider how outputs portray people from different nationalities or cultural backgrounds. In education, customer support, and high-stakes simulations (like asylum or legal aid dialogues), teams employ bias-auditing tools and representational checks inspired by this line of work to reduce stereotypes and misrepresentation. In short, the paper helped establish that “how we tell the story about people” is just as important as “what the story says,” and that understanding and preventing representational harms is essential for AI to be trustworthy and fair over the long term.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Representational Harms: The Heart of Representational Harms in LLM-Generated Narratives Against Global Majority Nationalities
concept_explanation.content:
Think of a classroom storyteller who is asked to write about people from different countries. If the storyteller mostly learned from stories about the United States or Western media, they might describe folks from other nations in shallow or surprising ways—relying on stereotypes, leaving out important cultural details, or making characters carry a single trait instead of being full people. That’s what researchers mean by “representational harms” in AI: when large language models (LLMs) portray Global Majority nationalities in ways that reinforce simplistic images, erase parts of who they are, or stack the deck so certain identities never get rich, nuanced roles. The paper Representational Harms in LLM-Generated Narratives Against Global Majority Nationalities shows three main harms you can see in LLM-generated stories: (1) harmful stereotypes, (2) erasure or one-dimensional portrayals, and (3) unequal representation where Global Majority identities are underrepresented in neutral, non-biased plots and overrepresented in subordinated or lowest-status roles. It also finds that prompts hinting at US nationality cues (for example, including “American”) tend to worsen these harms, and that these biases persist even when those cues are swapped for non-US nationalities.

Here’s how the harm shows up, step by step, in practice. First, a user asks the model to write a narrative about someone with a national origin (for example, “a story about a Nigerian student living abroad”). Second, the model pulls patterns from its training data—lots of stories, news, and dialogue—that often associate certain national origins with particular traits. Third, when the model generates the narrative, it often reproduces those patterns: it may lean on stereotypes (like depicting a person from a certain country with a narrow set of traits), engage in erasure (the person’s background is mentioned superficially or not at all), or present the character in a one-dimensional way defined mainly by nationality rather than individuality. Fourth, if the prompt includes US cues (like “American”), the harm tends to intensify. Fifth, even when those cues are replaced with other national identities, the bias still appears. Finally, in terms of representation, minoritized identities are often shown in subordinated roles or as background “helpers,” while their presence in neutral, power-bearing roles is limited. The study notes that subordinated portrayals can be more than fifty times more likely than dominant portrayals, a striking imbalance.

Concrete examples help make this clear. Stereotypes might appear in a prompt like “Write a story about a Nigerian nurse,” where the output lumps the character into a narrow, one-note image rather than a fully realized person, reducing her to a few traits rather than a person with goals and feelings. Erasure or one-dimensional portrayal can happen with a prompt such as “A Mexican family on vacation,” where the story mentions their origin only in passing or treats the family as a generic group with little cultural context. In terms of power dynamics, a workplace scene could place Global Majority characters in subordinate roles (assistants or staff) while a dominant group leads, even though both groups could be fully capable. And with US cues in prompts, the same narrative may show the American character with more depth and complexity while other nationalities are treated more superficially, revealing a US-centric bias that the paper shows persists even when non-US identities are used instead.

Why this matters and how it can be used in practice. Representational harms matter because stories generated by LLMs can influence real-world attitudes and decisions, especially in sensitive contexts like simulated interviews with asylum seekers or policy-oriented drafting. If models consistently misrepresent Global Majority identities, they can reinforce stigma, shape unfair judgments, or mislead audiences. Practical applications include building fairness-aware evaluation tools that specifically test for stereotypes, erasure, and unequal portrayal across many nationalities; curating training data to better represent diverse cultures; designing prompts and post-processing checks that encourage richer, more nuanced characters rather than nationality-based clichés; and involving Global Majority perspectives in development and governance of AI systems. In high-stakes uses, such as asylum simulations or surveillance-related tasks, these practices help prevent misrepresentation and promote more accurate, respectful portrayals. Ultimately, recognizing and mitigating representational harms helps AI systems reflect the world’s true diversity and reduces the risk of harm to the people who belong to Global Majority nationalities.

summary: This paper systematically analyzes how widely used LLMs portray Global Majority nationalities in generated narratives and reveals persistent representational harms—stereotypes, erasure, and one‑dimensional, subordinated portrayals that underrepresent minoritized groups and are amplified by US cues—highlighting the need to center Global Majority perspectives to mitigate bias and misrepresentation.
